title: Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum

herb_names_common.name: géranium à feuilles de clover atlantique (trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum)

what_description: Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum is a subspecies of flowering plant in the family Fabaceae. It is an annual herb with a compact growth habit, reaching heights of 10-30 cm, and has a hairy stem. The leaves are trifoliate, with leaflets 5-10 mm long and 2-4 mm wide, and the inflorescence is a dense spike of 10-20 pink to purple flowers. Each flower is 5-6 mm long and has 5-6 petals, while the fruit is a flat, hairy pod 3-5 mm long and containing 2-3 seeds. Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum is endemic to the western Iberian Peninsula, where it grows in open, dry places.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum are inflammation, which it reduces through its anti-inflammatory properties, and digestive issues, such as diarrhea, which it alleviates by regulating gut motility. Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum also helps to manage diabetes by lowering blood sugar levels due to its antidiabetic compounds. Additionally, it is effective in reducing hypertension through its vasodilatory properties, and it has been shown to exhibit antimicrobial activity against gastroenteritis-causing pathogens. The plant's wound-healing properties make it useful in treating skin irritation, wounds, and urinary tract infections, while its antifungal compounds combat fungal infections. Furthermore, Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum has been found to alleviate symptoms of asthma, cough, and anemia, as well as to reduce the severity of rheumatism, insect bites, and cardiovascular disease, including high blood pressure and gastritis, and arthritis.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum are antioxidant, which is attributed to the presence of flavonoids that can scavenge free radicals and protect cells from oxidative damage. The bioactive compounds in this plant, such as saponins, exhibit antimicrobial properties, inhibiting the growth of various pathogens, including bacteria and fungi, and preventing the spread of infections. Furthermore, the plant's anti-inflammatory properties are mediated by the action of volatile oils, which can reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with various conditions. Additionally, the cardioprotective effects of Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum are linked to the presence of phenolic acids, which can help prevent cardiovascular diseases by improving lipid profiles and reducing blood pressure. The plant's antifungal properties are also noteworthy, as they are attributed to the action of terpenoids, which can inhibit the growth of fungi and prevent the development of fungal infections.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum are flavonoids such as kaempferol and quercetin, which have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties by scavenging free radicals and inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes. Additionally, saponins have been identified in this species, which may contribute to its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities by disrupting cell membranes and inhibiting the growth of microorganisms. Phenolic acids including gallic acid and caffeic acid have also been detected, which are known for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties by scavenging free radicals and inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes. Coumarins such as scopoletin have been isolated, which may exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and disrupting cell membranes. Furthermore, alkaloids including trifolium have been identified, which may contribute to its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities by inhibiting the growth of microorganisms and disrupting cell membranes.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum are infusion, tea, tincture, decoction and salve. Infusion is made by steeping fresh or dried leaves in hot water to extract its bioactive compounds, which have been traditionally used to treat gastrointestinal issues and as a diuretic. Decoction is a stronger preparation made by boiling the leaves in water to release their cell wall-bound constituents, which have been studied for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Tincture is a concentrated liquid extract of the leaves in a solvent such as ethanol, often used in traditional medicine to treat fever, cough and digestive problems. Salve is a topical preparation made by infusing the leaves in a fat such as olive oil, often used to treat skin issues and wounds due to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties.
side_effects_description: The possible side effect of using Trifolium gemellum subsp. atlanticum are experience allergic reaction due to its high concentration of certain allergens like proteins and glycoproteins, causes vomiting as the plant's alkaloids can stimulate the vomiting center in the brain, experience nausea as the plant's toxic compounds can irritate the digestive system, trigger allergic reaction in individuals sensitive to certain compounds found in the plant, and lead to dehydration due to excessive diarrhea or vomiting resulting from consuming the plant.
